content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyzeProvider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json).
  6. Call setProviderTemplate with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

</object>

**URL Structure** (from the skip-to-content link):
```
https://www.autotrader.co.uk/car-search?channel=cars&postcode=ws149el&make=Porsche&model=911&homeDeliveryAdverts=include&advertising-location=at_cars&year-to=2026
```

**Query parameters observed:**
- `channel` = cars (fixed)
- `postcode` = user location
- `make` = car make (e.g., Porsche)


- `model` = car model (e.g., 911)
- `homeDeliveryAdverts` = include (fixed)
- `advertising-location` = at_cars (fixed)
- `year-to` = max year
- `page` = page number (seen in detail links)
- `sort` = relevance (seen in detail links)

The available filter pills include make, model, max year manufactured, price, mileage, gearbox, body type, fuel type, engine size, and distance—all of which map to corresponding URL parameters I can use to construct search queries.

I'm also seeing additional parameters for price ranges, year ranges, maximum mileage, transmission type, body type, fuel type, and engine size bounds. For the listing structure, each car is wrapped in an `<li>` element with a test ID following the `id-{advertId}` pattern, and I can extract the title from a link within each listing.

The listings are contained in a `ul` with class `sc-mddoqs-0`, and each `<li>` has class `sc-mddoqs-1`. I need to find a more stable selector for the price since the class names are dynamically generated. I'm noticing the images are lazy-loaded with placeholder divs, so I won't be able to extract them from the HTML directly. For the search schema, I can identify the key filter parameters from the URL structure: make, model, postcode, year range, price range, and a few other fields that are being cut off. the price span seems to be nested within a div, but all the class names appear to be dynamically generated, so I need to find a more reliable selector. Looking at the HTML structure, the price is consistently a span element within a specific div hierarchy, though without a data-testid attribute to anchor on. Let me look for a structural pattern that will be stable across different listings. I'm noticing the price element appears within the title link's hidden span alongside the car details, so I could extract it from there instead of relying on unstable CSS classes. Since the styled-component classes are generated and likely to change, I'll use the structural relationship with the title link as a more reliable fallback approach. I think the class-based selector approach is more practical—using `span.sc-1mc7cl3-14` to target the price span directly. searchSchema.description: The typed search fields as a JSON Schema (draft 2020-12) object, serialized to a JSON string.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyzeProvider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json).
  6. Call setProviderTemplate with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.commerce.Provider</typename>
</object>

- `transmission` - gearbox type

The listings have a consistent `data-testid` pattern that starts with "id-", so I can use that to reliably select them. Writing the schema and mappings... I need to include those fixed parameters in the URL template since the query mapping only handles dynamic fields. content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyzeProvider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json). The page source begins with a
     "Repeating elements" summary listing candidate container selectors with their frequency;
     use the top candidate (the element that wraps the fields and repeats once per result) as
     the itemLocator, and prefer the exact selector shown — e.g. a "^=" prefix selector when
     container ids are indexed (advertCard-0, advertCard-1), not an exact match.
  6. Call setProviderTemplate with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.commerce.Provider</typename>
</object>
Analyze the AutoTrader provider in your context and generate and persist its search template (search schema, request mapping, result mapping) by calling analyzeProvider then setProviderTemplate.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyzeProvider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json). The page source begins with a
     "Repeating elements" summary listing candidate container selectors with their frequency;
     use the top candidate (the element that wraps the fields and repeats once per result) as
     the itemLocator, and prefer the exact selector shown — e.g. a "^=" prefix selector when
     container ids are indexed (advertCard-0, advertCard-1), not an exact match.
  6. Call setProviderTemplate with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.commerce.Provider</typename>
</object>
Analyze the AutoTrader provider in your context and generate and persist its search template (search schema, request mapping, result mapping) by calling analyzeProvider then setProviderTemplate.

- `sort` - string (e.g., "relevance")

From the filter pills visible on the page, I can identify additional parameters for make, model, year range, price range, mileage, gearbox, body type, fuel type, engine size, and distance filtering.

Looking at the repeating card elements, `[data-advertid]` appears on each of the 24 listings and wraps around 17 fields per card. For extracting individual fields, the title and URL both come from the `a[data-testid="search-listing-title"]` element—the text content gives me the title while the href attribute provides the link.

The price text appears within the card but I need to find a reliable way to target it without relying on fragile class names. I notice the price is consistently positioned at the end of the hidden span within the title anchor, formatted like "3.6 997 Carrera Cabriolet 2dr, £23,995", so I could parse it from there. The visible price display uses generated class names that aren't reliable across listings, so extracting from the title's hidden content might be the more stable approach, though it requires parsing the text to isolate the price value. I'll use the advertCard selector as the item locator since it's stable, though I'm realizing the images are likely loaded dynamically as background images rather than img tags. For the price, the hidden span within the title contains the subtitle text with the price appended, which is the most reliable approach I can find. Looking at the card structure more carefully, the price is nested several levels deep without a stable data-testid attribute, so I'll need to use a more specific CSS selector that targets the span containing the price value within the price block area. I can use the hidden span text within the search listing title to extract the price, though it'll be bundled with the subtitle and require parsing on the consumer's end. That's a pragmatic trade-off given the lack of a dedicated selector.

For now, I'll map the fields I can reliably target with data-testid attributes: title, URL, subtitle, mileage, year, and location. I'll skip the price for now since it doesn't have a clean selector.

Actually, let me reconsider the price field—I can extract it from the hidden span even if it's not ideal. And for the image, the carousel anchors don't have actual image URLs I can pull, so that's not viable either.

Let me finalize the template with the fields I can reliably extract... I'm realizing the title link's textContent actually captures everything I need—the car details and price are all concatenated together in that hidden span. So I can rely on just extracting the full text from the `[data-testid="search-listing-title"]` element rather than hunting for separate price selectors. Let me finalize the template with this approach.

Writing schema and request config...

I've inspected the AutoTrader UK page source. Here's what I found:

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyze-provider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json). The page source begins with a
     "Repeating elements" summary listing candidate container selectors with their frequency;
     use the top candidate (the element that wraps the fields and repeats once per result) as
     the itemLocator, and prefer the exact selector shown — e.g. a "^=" prefix selector when
     container ids are indexed (advertCard-0, advertCard-1), not an exact match.
  6. Call set-provider-template with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.commerce.Provider</typename>
</object>
